2604.27697.title: Deep Learning-Based Segmentation of Peritoneal Cancer Index Regions from CT Imaging
2604.27697.paper_summary: Peritoneal metastases are currently assessed using diagnostic laparoscopy to determine Sugarbaker's Peritoneal Cancer Index (sPCI), which works by dividing the abdomen into 13 regions and scoring each region based on tumor size. A recent consensus study defined 3D regions to facilitate a radiological PCI (rPCI), providing standardized anatomical regions for imaging-based assessment. Despite its clinical value, sPCI is invasive and lacks a standardized imaging counterpart. In this study, we propose a deep learning-based approach to automatically segment the rPCI regions on CT. We evaluate nnU-Net and Swin UNETR on 62 CT scans with rPCI regions manually annotated by three clinical researchers and validated by two expert radiologists. Performance was assessed using five-fold cross-validation with the Dice Similarity Coefficient (Dice), 95th percentile Hausdorff distance and Average Surface Distance. nnU-Net achieved an overall Dice of 0.82, approaching interobserver agreement (0.88) and outperforming Swin UNETR (0.76), with remaining challenges primarily in right flank and small-bowel regions. These results demonstrate feasibility of automated rPCI segmentation, laying the foundation for non-invasive, imaging-based assessment.
2604.27697.paper_summary_zh: 腹膜轉移目前使用診斷性腹腔鏡檢查來評估 Sugarbaker 的腹膜癌指數 (sPCI)，該指數通過將腹部劃分為 13 個區域並根據腫瘤大小對每個區域進行打分來運作。最近的一項共識研究定義了 3D 區域，以促進放射學 PCI (rPCI)，為基於影像的評估提供標準化的解剖區域。儘管 sPCI 具有臨床價值，但它是侵入性的，並且缺乏標準化的影像對應物。在這項研究中，我們提出了一種基於深度學習的方法，自動分割 CT 上的 rPCI 區域。我們在 62 例 CT 掃描上評估了 nnU-Net 和 Swin UNETR，這些掃描的 rPCI 區域由三位臨床研究人員手動標註，並由兩位專家放射科醫生驗證。性能評估使用了五折交叉驗證，並計算了 Dice 相似度係數 (Dice)、第 95 百分位的 Hausdorff 距離和平均表面距離。nnU-Net 的 Dice 總體達到 0.82，接近觀察者間一致性 (0.88)，並且優於 Swin UNETR (0.76)，剩餘的挑戰主要集中在右側腹部和小腸區域。這些結果顯示了自動化 rPCI 分割的可行性，為非侵入性、基於影像的評估奠定了基礎。
2604.27697.author: Pieter C. Gort et.al.
2604.27697.authors: Pieter C. Gort, Lotte J. S. Ewals, Marion W. Tops-Welten, Cris H. B. Claessens, Joost Nederend, Fons van der Sommen
